11: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting significantly enhances LLMs complex reasoning
12: abilities by mimicking a step-by-step thought process (see Fig. 1). Instead of providing
13: a direct answer, CoT prompts guide the model to generate a sequence of intermediate
14: reasoning steps. This explicit breakdown allows LLMs to tackle complex problems by
15: decomposing them into smaller, more manageable sub-problems. This technique
16: markedly improves the model's performance on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning,
17: such as arithmetic, common sense reasoning, and symbolic manipulation. A primary
18: advantage of CoT is its ability to transform a difficult, single-step problem into a series
19: of simpler steps, thereby increasing the transparency of the LLM's reasoning process.
20: This approach not only boosts accuracy but also offers valuable insights into the
21: model's decision-making, aiding in debugging and comprehension. CoT can be
22: implemented using various strategies, including offering few-shot examples that
23: demonstrate step-by-step reasoning or simply instructing the model to "think step by
24: step." Its effectiveness stems from its ability to guide the model's internal processing
25: toward a more deliberate and logical progression. As a result, Chain-of-Thought has
26: become a cornerstone technique for enabling advanced reasoning capabilities in
27: contemporary LLMs. This enhanced transparency and breakdown of complex
28: problems into manageable sub-problems is particularly important for autonomous
29: agents, as it enables them to perform more reliable and auditable actions in complex
